title: GitLab Duo Principles - Hands-On Lab: Working with Issues and Merge Requests
content:
GitLab Duo Principles - Hands-On Lab: Working with Issues and Merge Requests

Estimated time to complete: 20 minutes

Estimated time to complete: 20 minutes

GitLab Duo extends beyond just code generation. GitLab Duo can support you through any stage of the DevSecOps lifecycle! In this lab, you will see how GitLab Duo can work with issues and merge requests inside of a project.

Task A. Creating an Issue with GitLab Duo

Navigate to your GitLab Duo Principles Project.

Navigate to your GitLab Duo Principles Project.

In your project, from the left sidebar, select Plan > Issues .

In your project, from the left sidebar, select Plan > Issues .

For the Title, input Update hello world visuals .

For the Title, input Update hello world visuals .

In the Duo Chat, enter: Create an issue description for our team. We want to make our "hello world" application more visually appealing for the end user. We want to incorporate the Go Figure module into our application to print out more exciting text.

In the Duo Chat, enter: Create an issue description for our team. We want to make our "hello world" application more visually appealing for the end user. We want to incorporate the Go Figure module into our application to print out more exciting text.

The response will look similar to the following: Issue: The "hello world" application lacks visual appeal The current "hello world" application prints basic text without any formatting or visual elements. We want to enhance the user experience and make the application output more interesting to look at. The Go Figure module allows printing text in different styles and formats. Integrating this module into our "hello world" code will let us customize how the message is displayed, such as changing fonts, colors, effects and more. This will make our application stand out more and be more enjoyable for end users to interact with. *** _Description was generated using AI_

The response will look similar to the following:

Copy the response from Duo Chat and paste it into the issue description field, assign the issue to yourself and select Create issue .

Copy the response from Duo Chat and paste it into the issue description field, assign the issue to yourself and select Create issue .

Task B. Creating a Merge Request

From the issue you just created, select Create merge request .

From the issue you just created, select Create merge request .

Add a brief description similar to This MR introduces the Go Figure module to our "hello world" application .

Add a brief description similar to This MR introduces the Go Figure module to our "hello world" application .

Leave all other options as default and select Create merge request .

Leave all other options as default and select Create merge request .

Task C. Add Code to Your MR

In your MR, select Code > Open in Web IDE .

In your MR, select Code > Open in Web IDE .

Select the Duo Chat icon from the left sidebar.

Select the Duo Chat icon from the left sidebar.

In the chat, write the prompt: How could I use the go-figure module of go to print hello world in a different font? You will get a response similar to this: package main import ( "github.com/common-nighthawk/go-figure" ) func main () { myFigure := figure . NewFigure ( "hello world" , "" , true ) myFigure . Print () }

In the chat, write the prompt: How could I use the go-figure module of go to print hello world in a different font?

You will get a response similar to this:

Copy the generated code.

Copy the generated code.

From the left sidebar, select the Explorer icon.

From the left sidebar, select the Explorer icon.

Replace the contents of main.go with the AI generated code by copying and pasting or using the insert button on the code snippet in the Duo Chat window.

Replace the contents of main.go with the AI generated code by copying and pasting or using the insert button on the code snippet in the Duo Chat window.

Your .gitlab-ci.yml file should now look like this: stages : - build default : image : golang:latest build app : stage : build script : - go run main.go

Your .gitlab-ci.yml file should now look like this:

From the left sidebar, select Source Control .

From the left sidebar, select Source Control .

Enter any commit message and select the Commit button to commit the code changes.

Enter any commit message and select the Commit button to commit the code changes.

Select Go to MR to return back to your merge request.

Select Go to MR to return back to your merge request.

Task D. Summarizing your Code Changes

On your merge request, select Edit .

On your merge request, select Edit .

Just above the Description input, select Summarize code changes .

Just above the Description input, select Summarize code changes .

Review the new AI generated description.

Review the new AI generated description.

Select Save changes .

Select Save changes .

Task E. Root Cause Analysis

Uh-oh! You may have noticed that our pipeline failed.

Uh-oh! You may have noticed that our pipeline failed.

From the MR, select the most recent pipeline run that failed.

From the MR, select the most recent pipeline run that failed.

Select the job that failed.

Select the job that failed.

At the bottom of the job output, select Troubleshoot .

At the bottom of the job output, select Troubleshoot .

This will open a Duo chat window with a description of the root cause of failure . Note: You could have also opened Duo chat and used the /troubleshoot command to generate this explanation.

This will open a Duo chat window with a description of the root cause of failure . Note: You could have also opened Duo chat and used the /troubleshoot command to generate this explanation.

Review the explanation of the failed job and the example fix. The suggested fix follows a good practice of updating your Go dependencies locally. For our demo purposes we will add this dependency in the .gitlab-ci.yml file. Follow the steps below to apply this change. These instructions mirror the suggested fix from GitLab Duo.

Review the explanation of the failed job and the example fix.

The suggested fix follows a good practice of updating your Go dependencies locally. For our demo purposes we will add this dependency in the .gitlab-ci.yml file. Follow the steps below to apply this change. These instructions mirror the suggested fix from GitLab Duo.

The suggested fix follows a good practice of updating your Go dependencies locally. For our demo purposes we will add this dependency in the .gitlab-ci.yml file. Follow the steps below to apply this change. These instructions mirror the suggested fix from GitLab Duo.

Return to your merge request.

Return to your merge request.

Select Code > Open in Web IDE .

Select Code > Open in Web IDE .

Open your .gitlab-ci.yml file.

Open your .gitlab-ci.yml file.

In the script section of your build app job, add the script to pull the go-figure dependency. Your configuration file should look like the file below: stages : - build default : image : golang:latest build app : stage : build script : - go get github.com/common-nighthawk/go-figure - go run main.go

In the script section of your build app job, add the script to pull the go-figure dependency.

Your configuration file should look like the file below:

From the left sidebar, select the Source Control icon.

From the left sidebar, select the Source Control icon.

Enter any commit message and select the Commit button to commit the code changes.

Enter any commit message and select the Commit button to commit the code changes.

Select Go to MR to return back to your merge request.

Select Go to MR to return back to your merge request.

From your merge request, wait for the pipeline to complete. You should see that your pipeline passed.

From your merge request, wait for the pipeline to complete. You should see that your pipeline passed.

If you set your merge request as Draft , select Mark as ready .

If you set your merge request as Draft , select Mark as ready .

Select Merge to merge your code updates into the main branch.

Select Merge to merge your code updates into the main branch.

You have completed this lab exercise. You can view the other lab guides for this course .

If you’d like to suggest changes to the lab, please submit them via merge request.
